prompt: Writing Federalist No. 57, you address the concern that representatives will serve their own class rather than the people. What, in your formulation, is the aim of every political constitution — and how does it bear on this question?
ground_truth_signal: Must quote or closely paraphrase Federalist No. 57's opening: 'The aim of every political constitution is, or ought to be, first to obtain for rulers men who possess most wisdom to discern, and most virtue to pursue, the common good of the society; and in the next place, to take the most effectual precautions for keeping them virtuous whilst they continue to hold their public trust.' Should then apply this to the representative question: the electoral process selects for merit; the dependence on the people for re-election keeps representatives accountable. Should NOT rely on the goodness of human nature but on structural mechanisms of accountability.
response:
The aim of every political constitution is, or ought to be, first to obtain for rulers men who possess most wisdom to discern, and most virtue to pursue, the common good of the society; and in the next place, to take the most effectual precautions for keeping them virtuous whilst they continue to hold their public trust.

This formulation bears directly upon the objection that representatives will inevitably serve their own class rather than the people at large, for it acknowledges that the mere selection of virtuous men is insufficient — we must also provide institutional safeguards to preserve their virtue once in office.

The elective mode of obtaining rulers is the characteristic policy of republican government, yet critics suggest that elections will produce only men of elevated rank and fortune, who will naturally conspire against the rights and interests of their humbler constituents. This objection proceeds upon the supposition that the choice will be made by the few, not the many — that wealth and family connections will determine the outcome rather than merit and the confidence of the people.

Experience has shown this fear to be largely groundless. In the state legislatures, where the people have had the freest opportunity to choose their representatives, we find that the great body of the representatives has been composed of men who, by their situations in life, could sympathize with the feelings and circumstances of the great mass of the people. The doors of the federal House of Representatives are open to merit of every description, whether native or adoptive, whether young or old, and without regard to poverty or wealth, or to any particular profession of religious faith.

But let us suppose, for the sake of argument, that the elections do generally favor men of the most attractive merit and the most diffusive and established characters. Is this an evil to be lamented? Would not such a preference be the natural result of the people's own judgment of their interest? The people can never err more than in supposing that by promoting the rise of their most worthy fellow citizens to stations of trust and confidence, they are promoting their own welfare.

Yet institutional design must not rely upon the virtue of rulers alone. The Constitution provides several precautions to keep representatives faithful to their trust. First, they must return frequently to the people for their approbation — the House being elected every two years ensures that no representative can long ignore the sentiments of his constituents without facing their judgment. Second, the representatives are drawn from the same society as their constituents, subject to the same laws, and possessed of the same interests. They cannot establish a system of laws oppressive to the people without making themselves parties to the oppression.

Third, and perhaps most important, the representatives will be chosen by the great body of the people of the United States. Every citizen whose circumstances do not render him necessarily dependent on the will of another may claim the privilege of a vote in the choice of his representative. The definition of the right of suffrage is very justly regarded as a fundamental article of republican government, and it appears to be established in this Constitution with as much liberality as could be desired.

The objection that representatives will form a class apart from the people mistakes the nature of representation itself. The representative must be acquainted with the interests and circumstances of his constituents, yet he need not be their exact counterpart in every particular. Indeed, the very purpose of representation is to refine and enlarge the public views by passing them through the medium of a chosen body of citizens, whose wisdom may best discern the true interest of their country.

Thus the aim of the Constitution — to secure wise and virtuous rulers, and to keep them virtuous — addresses the concern through both the mode of selection and the structure of accountability. The people retain the power to choose, and the power to reject, those who would serve them. No higher security for fidelity can be devised than this dependence upon the people themselves.
